title: Hello, C++
successMessage: Welcome to C++. That #include pulls in the input/output library. 'using namespace std' lets you write cout instead of std::cout. return 0 signals the program succeeded.

title: Print multiple lines

codeChecks.message: Add two more cout statements — three total.
Replace the TODO with your code.

successMessage: Three outputs — C++ runs them top to bottom. Every cout ends with endl (or '\n' for speed — endl flushes the buffer, '\n' doesn't).

title: \n vs endl
instruction:
`endl` flushes the output buffer — safe but slightly slower.
`'\n'` just adds a newline — faster, preferred in performance-critical code.

For learning they're the same. In game engines and servers, `'\n'` is the choice.
successMessage: Both work for learning. For production: '\n' in tight loops, endl when you need output to appear immediately (like progress reporting). Now you know the difference.

title: Comments
instruction:
C++ has two comment styles:

- `// single line` — everything after `//`
- `/* multi-line */` — spans lines

The preprocessor ignores them. Comments explain WHY — not what.
successMessage: Two comment styles. // for quick notes, /* */ for longer explanations. Great code is self-documenting — comments should explain reasoning, not repeat the code.

title: cin — read user input
instruction:
`cin` (console in) reads from the keyboard. `>>` is the stream extraction operator:

```cpp
int age;
cin >> age;  // reads an integer
```

In our sandbox, `cin` is pre-filled. Run this to see how input/output works together.
successMessage: (x + y) / 2.0 — the .0 makes it a double division. Without it, 35/2 = 17 (integer division). This is one of C++'s classic beginner traps.

title: Arithmetic operators
instruction:
C++ arithmetic:

- `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` — standard math
- `%` — modulo (remainder)
- `5 / 2 = 2` — **integer division** — drops the decimal!
- `5.0 / 2 = 2.5` — make one a double for decimal result
- `int(3.9) = 3` — cast truncates, doesn't round
successMessage: 10/3 = 3 — integer division truncates. 10.0/3 = 3.333... — one double operand forces decimal division. (int)3.99 = 3 — C-style cast truncates. These rules trip up beginners constantly.

title: Write it from scratch
instruction:
No starter code. Write everything yourself.

Print exactly:
```
C++ is compiled.
C++ is fast.
C++ is powerful.
Chapter 1 complete!
```

Four `cout` lines, all with `endl`.
hint: #include <iostream>\nusing namespace std;\nint main() { cout << "..." << endl; return 0; }

codeChecks.message: Define the main function: int main()

successMessage: Written from memory — #include, namespace, main, four couts, return 0. That skeleton becomes second nature within a few chapters. Chapter 1 done!
